eel: noun. Any freshwater fish of the order Anguilliformes, which are elongated and resemble snakes.
elk: noun. Any of various large species of deer such as the red deer, moose or wapiti (see usage notes).
else: adjective. Other; in addition to previously mentioned items.
ewe: noun. A female sheep, as opposed to a ram.
isle: noun. An island, usually very small.
keel: noun. A large beam along the underside of a ship’s hull from bow to stern.
kiwi: noun. A flightless bird of the order Apterygiformes native to New Zealand.
lee: noun. A protected cove or harbor, out of the wind.
leek: noun. A vegetable of variety Allium ampeloprasum, having edible leaves and an onion -like bulb but with a milder flavour than the onion.
lees: noun. The sediment that settles during fermentation of beverages, consisting of dead yeast and precipitated parts of the fruit.
lei: noun. A garland of flowers in Hawaii.
lek: noun. An aggregation of male animals for the purposes of courtship and display.
lewis: noun. A cramp iron inserted into a cavity in order to lift heavy stones; used as a symbol of strength in Freemasonry.
lie: verb. To rest in a horizontal position on a surface.
like: verb. To enjoy, be pleased by; favor; be in favor of.
lwei: noun. a monetary unit of Angola used from 1975-1990, one hundredth of a kwanza.
see: verb. To perceive or detect someone or something with the eyes, or as if by sight.
seek: verb. To try to find; to look for; to search for.
sel: symbol. ISO 639-2 & ISO 639-3 language code for Selkup.
sew: verb. To use a needle to pass thread repeatedly through (pieces of fabric) in order to join them together.
sike: noun. A gutter or ditch; a small stream that frequently dries up in the summer; the marshy ground or ditch through which such a stream
silk: noun. A fine fiber excreted by the silkworm or other arthropod (such as a spider).
skew: verb. To form or shape in an oblique way; to cause to take an oblique position.
ski: noun. One of a pair of long flat runners designed for gliding over snow or water.
sleek: adjective. Having an even, smooth surface; smooth
slew: noun. The act, or process of slewing.
week: noun. Any period of seven consecutive days.
weel: noun. A trap for catching fish; a weely.
wile: noun. A trick or stratagem practiced for ensnaring or deception; a sly, insidious artifice
wise: adjective. Showing good judgement or the benefit of experience.
eek: interjection. Representing a scream or shriek (especially in comic strips and books).
eeks: verb. third-person singular simple present indicative of eek
eli: symbol. ISO 639-3 language code for Nding.
ike: symbol. ISO 639-3 language code for Eastern Canadian Inuktitut.
kie: noun. Alternative spelling of kye, plural of cow
ksi: symbol. ISO 639-3 language code for Krisa.
lese: verb. Alternative form of leese (“to lose”)
lew: noun. A French gold coin circulated in 15th- century Scotland.
selkie: noun. A seal which can magically transform into a human by shedding its skin.
sewel: noun. A scarecrow, generally made of feathers tied to a string, hung up to prevent deer from breaking into a place.
sil: noun. A yellowish pigment used by painters in ancient times.
silkie: noun. A chicken of a certain breed with very fine, silk-like feathers, blue-black skin and bones, and five toes per foot (instead of the
skeel: noun. A shallow wooden vessel for holding milk or cream.
swee: noun. A finch of species Coccopygia melanotis, found in sub-Saharan Africa.
weki: noun. Alternative form of wheki (“the tree fern Dicksonia squarrosa”).
welk: verb. Of a plant: to wither, wilt, decay.
wiki: noun. A collaborative website which can be directly edited merely by using a web browser, often by anyone who has access to it.
wiselike: adjective. Resembling that which is wise or sensible; judicious; sensible.
